assessment.title: AI Automation Engineer Onboarding Assessment
assessment.description: An adaptive placement assessment for AI Automation Engineers. This bank contains 100 questions (10 per skill). During onboarding, 2 questions per skill are randomly selected to produce a unique 20-question assessment for each student. This is not a certification exam.

selection.note: Randomly draw 2 questions from each skill's 10-question pool at onboarding time to build a unique assessment per student.

assessment.instructions: Select one option for each question. The correct answer and its explanation are revealed only after you click an option. Answer honestly; this test places you on the roadmap and is not graded as a pass/fail exam.

questions.question: Which Python library is most commonly used to send an HTTP request to an API?
questions.explanation: The requests library is the standard way to make HTTP calls to APIs in Python.

questions.question: Which Python type best represents a JSON object from an API?
questions.explanation: A dict maps naturally to a JSON object of key-value pairs.

questions.question: Which loop processes each item in a list of records?
questions.explanation: A for loop iterates over each element of a list.

questions.question: Why wrap repeated automation logic in a function?
options: To reuse it and keep code organized
questions.explanation: Functions make logic reusable and code easier to maintain.

questions.question: Which module parses a JSON string into a Python object?
questions.explanation: The json module's loads() converts a JSON string into Python data.

questions.question: Which value represents 'true' in Python?
questions.explanation: Python's boolean literal is True (capital T).

questions.question: How do you output a value to the console in Python?
questions.explanation: print() writes to standard output in Python.

questions.question: Given data as a dict, how do you safely read key 'email' without crashing if missing?
questions.explanation: data.get("email") returns None instead of raising KeyError when the key is absent.

questions.question: An external API may fail intermittently. Which construct handles that gracefully?
questions.explanation: try/except catches exceptions so a failed call doesn't crash the automation.

questions.question: Where should an API key used by a Python script be stored?
options: In an environment variable read at runtime
questions.explanation: Secrets belong in environment variables, not hard-coded or committed.

questions.question: Which HTTP method typically creates a new record?
questions.explanation: POST sends data to create a new resource.

questions.question: Which HTTP method retrieves data without changing it?
questions.explanation: GET reads data and should not modify server state.

questions.question: Which HTTP method removes a resource?
questions.explanation: DELETE removes the specified resource.

questions.question: What is an API 'endpoint'?
options: A specific URL that exposes a resource or action
questions.explanation: An endpoint is a URL you call to access a specific API resource or operation.

questions.question: In a POST request, the body usually contains...
options: The input data being sent to the server
questions.explanation: The request body carries the data you send to be processed or stored.

questions.question: What are HTTP headers commonly used for?
options: Carrying metadata like Content-Type and Authorization
questions.explanation: Headers carry metadata such as content type and auth tokens.

questions.question: Which status code range indicates a successful request?
questions.explanation: 2xx codes (e.g. 200, 201) indicate success.

questions.question: Your automation gets a 401 response. What does it usually mean?
options: Authentication failed / invalid or missing key
questions.explanation: 401 Unauthorized means authentication failed, often a bad or missing API key.

questions.question: You receive HTTP 429. What is the best response in an automation?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop
Back off and retry after a delay
questions.explanation: 429 means too many requests; back off and retry after a delay (respect Retry-After).

questions.question: Why does GET being 'idempotent' matter for automations?
options: Repeating a GET has the same effect and is safe to retry
questions.explanation: Idempotent GETs can be safely retried without side effects.

questions.question: Which is a valid JSON object?
questions.explanation: JSON keys and string values must use double quotes.

questions.question: Which is NOT a valid JSON value type?
questions.explanation: JSON supports strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays, and objects, not functions.

questions.question: How is an array written in JSON?
questions.explanation: JSON arrays use square brackets: [1, 2, 3].

questions.question: Why is JSON widely used in automations?
options: It is a lightweight, standard format APIs exchange data in
questions.explanation: JSON is a compact, language-neutral format for data exchange between systems.

questions.question: How do you represent 'no value' in JSON?
questions.explanation: JSON uses null for the absence of a value.

questions.question: How must text values be wrapped in JSON?
questions.explanation: JSON strings must use double quotes.

questions.question: How is a boolean written in JSON?
questions.explanation: JSON booleans are lowercase true and false.

questions.question: For { "user": { "name": "Ada" } }, which path gets the name in most tools?
questions.explanation: Dot notation user.name drills into nested objects.

questions.question: For items: ["a", "b", "c"], how do you reference "b" in most automation tools?
questions.explanation: Arrays are zero-indexed, so index 1 is "b".

questions.question: What does it mean to 'parse' JSON in a workflow?
options: Convert the JSON text into structured data you can map from
questions.explanation: Parsing turns a JSON string into structured fields your steps can reference.

questions.question: What is a webhook?
options: An automatic message an app sends to a URL when an event happens
A tool that only sends email
questions.explanation: A webhook is an event-driven HTTP callback sent to a URL when something occurs.

questions.question: Webhooks usually deliver data via which HTTP method?
questions.explanation: Webhooks typically POST the event payload to your endpoint.

questions.question: What does a webhook payload contain?
options: Data describing the event that occurred
questions.explanation: The payload carries details about the event (usually as JSON).

questions.question: To receive a webhook, you must provide the sender with...
options: A URL endpoint that can accept the request
questions.explanation: You register a URL where the sender POSTs event data.

questions.question: In an automation, a webhook typically acts as the...
questions.explanation: An incoming webhook is a common trigger that starts a workflow instantly.

questions.question: A benefit of webhooks over scheduled checks is that they are...
options: Near real time when the event happens
questions.explanation: Webhooks fire the instant an event occurs, unlike periodic polling.

questions.question: What should your endpoint return so the sender knows it was received?
questions.explanation: Returning a 2xx status acknowledges successful receipt.

questions.question: Why is a webhook often better than polling for reacting to events?
options: It fires in near real time instead of checking on a schedule
Polling never misses but webhooks always do
questions.explanation: Webhooks push data immediately; polling adds delay and wasted calls.

questions.question: How can you verify a webhook really came from the expected sender?
options: Validate a signature/secret (e.g. HMAC) on the request
Only check the time of day
questions.explanation: Signature verification (e.g. HMAC with a shared secret) confirms authenticity.

questions.question: If your endpoint is briefly down, well-designed senders will usually...
questions.explanation: Many providers retry webhook delivery with backoff if the endpoint fails.

questions.question: In n8n, what is a 'node'?
options: A single step in a workflow that performs an action
questions.explanation: A node is one step (trigger, action, or logic) in an n8n workflow.

questions.question: In n8n, what is a 'workflow'?
options: A series of connected nodes that automate a process
questions.explanation: A workflow is connected nodes forming the automation.

questions.question: What starts an n8n workflow?
questions.explanation: A trigger node (webhook, cron, app event) starts the workflow.

questions.question: Which n8n node calls any external REST API?
questions.explanation: The HTTP Request node lets you call arbitrary REST APIs.

questions.question: A distinctive trait of n8n is that it is...
questions.explanation: n8n is open-source and can be self-hosted for data control.

questions.question: How does data move between n8n nodes?
options: Output items of one node feed into the next
questions.explanation: Each node's output items become the input of the next node.

questions.question: What are n8n expressions used for?
options: Referencing data from earlier nodes dynamically
questions.explanation: Expressions pull values from prior nodes into fields dynamically.

questions.question: A benefit of self-hosting n8n is...
options: Control over data and no per-task cloud fees
questions.explanation: Self-hosting keeps data in your control and avoids per-task pricing.

questions.question: What does the n8n Code node let you do?
options: Run custom JavaScript/Python for advanced logic
questions.explanation: The Code node runs custom JS/Python for logic beyond built-in nodes.

questions.question: How can n8n handle a failed execution automatically?
options: With an error trigger / error workflow
questions.explanation: An error workflow (error trigger) runs when an execution fails, e.g. to alert you.

questions.question: In Make.com, what is a 'scenario'?
options: An automated workflow of connected modules
questions.explanation: A scenario is Make's workflow: connected modules moving/transforming data.

questions.question: In Make.com, what is a 'module'?
options: A single step/action within a scenario
questions.explanation: A module is one action or step inside a scenario.

questions.question: What is the first module in a Make scenario usually?
options: A trigger that starts the scenario
questions.explanation: The first module is a trigger (instant or scheduled) that starts the scenario.

questions.question: How do you let Make access an app like Google Sheets?
questions.explanation: You create an authorized connection so Make can act on your behalf.

questions.question: What is 'mapping' in Make.com?
options: Passing data fields from one module into another
questions.explanation: Mapping wires output fields of one module into the inputs of the next.

questions.question: In Make pricing, an 'operation' is roughly...
options: One action performed by a module
questions.explanation: Each module action consumes an operation, which affects billing.

questions.question: What does a filter between modules do?
options: Lets data pass only when a condition is met
questions.explanation: Filters allow the flow to continue only when their condition is true.

questions.question: Which Make.com tool sends data down different branches by condition?
questions.explanation: A Router splits a scenario into branches; filters on each decide the path.

questions.question: What does an Iterator module do?
options: Splits an array into separate items processed one by one
questions.explanation: An Iterator breaks an array into individual bundles for per-item processing.

questions.question: What does an Aggregator do, opposite to an Iterator?
options: Combines multiple items back into one bundle/array
questions.explanation: An Aggregator merges multiple items into a single combined output.

questions.question: In Zapier, what starts a Zap?
questions.explanation: A Trigger is the event that starts a Zap; Actions run afterward.

questions.question: In Zapier, what is an 'Action'?
options: A task the Zap performs after the trigger
questions.explanation: An Action is a step the Zap runs after the trigger fires.

questions.question: What is a 'Zap'?
options: An automated workflow connecting apps (trigger + actions)
questions.explanation: A Zap is an automation: a trigger followed by one or more actions.

questions.question: A multi-step Zap can...
options: Run several actions after one trigger
questions.explanation: Multi-step Zaps chain multiple actions after a single trigger.

questions.question: Zapier primarily connects...
questions.explanation: Zapier integrates thousands of web apps to automate tasks between them.

questions.question: In Zapier billing, a 'task' is...
options: Each action a Zap successfully completes
questions.explanation: A task is counted each time a Zap action runs successfully.

questions.question: Before turning a Zap on, you should...
questions.explanation: Testing verifies each step works before the Zap runs live.

questions.question: You want a Zap to continue only if order total > $100. Which step?
questions.explanation: A Filter lets the Zap proceed only when its condition is met.

questions.question: Which Zapier feature runs different actions based on conditions (if/then branches)?
questions.explanation: Paths create conditional branches so different actions run for different cases.

questions.question: What is the Formatter by Zapier used for?
options: Transforming data (dates, text, numbers) between steps
questions.explanation: Formatter transforms values (format dates, split text, do math) between steps.

questions.question: When adding an AI step to an automation, why is a clear prompt important?
options: It guides the model to consistent, usable output
questions.explanation: Clear prompts yield consistent output the next step can use reliably.

questions.question: What is a prompt?
options: The instruction/input you give an AI model
questions.explanation: A prompt is the input text/instructions guiding the model.

questions.question: Telling the AI 'You are a support assistant that replies politely' is an example of...
questions.explanation: Assigning a role/persona steers tone and behavior.

questions.question: Which prompt is likely to give the most consistent result?
options: A specific prompt with clear instructions and format
questions.explanation: Specific instructions and a defined output format improve consistency.

questions.question: Adding example input/output pairs to a prompt helps because it...
options: Shows the model the exact pattern you want
questions.explanation: Examples demonstrate the desired format and improve reliability.

questions.question: Adding 'Answer in one sentence' to a prompt is an example of...
questions.explanation: Constraints bound the output (length, format) for downstream use.

questions.question: Why include relevant context (like the customer's message) in a prompt?
options: So the model can produce a relevant, grounded response
questions.explanation: Context grounds the model so answers fit the actual situation.

questions.question: To let the next module parse the AI result reliably, ask the model to output...
questions.explanation: Structured JSON lets downstream steps map each field reliably.

questions.question: Providing a couple of worked examples in the prompt is called...
questions.explanation: A few in-prompt examples is few-shot prompting.

questions.question: In an automation, how do you reduce wrong AI answers?
options: Ground the prompt in provided data and validate the output
questions.explanation: Grounding in supplied data plus output validation reduces incorrect actions.

questions.question: What makes an AI agent different from a single AI text step?
options: It can choose tools/actions and take steps toward a goal
It cannot use an AI model
questions.explanation: An agent reasons and calls tools in a loop, unlike a one-shot AI step.
questions.roadmap_reference: AI Agents > What Is an Agent

questions.question: In n8n's AI Agent node, a 'tool' you give the agent is...
options: A capability it can call (API, search, DB lookup)
questions.explanation: A tool is an action the agent can invoke to gather info or make changes.

questions.question: An agent uses an LLM mainly to...
options: Decide what to do next toward a goal
questions.explanation: The LLM is the agent's reasoning engine deciding the next action.

questions.question: Which is a good example of an agent task in automation?
options: Read an email, decide to reply/escalate, then act
questions.explanation: Agents shine at tasks needing decisions and actions, like triaging email.

questions.question: An agent typically works by...
options: Reasoning, acting with a tool, observing, and repeating
questions.explanation: Agents loop through reason-act-observe until the task is done.

questions.question: What does giving an agent memory allow?
options: Recalling earlier steps or conversation context
questions.explanation: Memory lets the agent remember prior steps/context during a task.

questions.question: Adding a human approval before an agent sends an external message is called...
questions.explanation: Human-in-the-loop inserts human review before risky actions.

questions.question: Why cap an agent's iterations or actions?
options: To prevent runaway loops and control cost
questions.explanation: Caps stop infinite loops and limit token/API cost.

questions.question: How does an agent decide which tool to use?
options: The LLM reasons about the task and selects an appropriate tool
questions.explanation: The LLM chooses tools based on the task and available tool descriptions.

questions.question: A key risk when an agent can take real actions is...
options: It may take an unintended action without guardrails
questions.explanation: Autonomous actions can go wrong without approvals, limits, and logging.

questions.question: What does 'if/then' logic do in an automation?
options: Runs different actions depending on a condition
questions.explanation: If/then branches run different actions based on whether a condition holds.

questions.question: Most automations follow which basic pattern?
options: Trigger, then one or more actions
questions.explanation: An event (trigger) starts the flow, then actions run in sequence.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a filter/condition step?
options: Allow the flow to continue only when criteria are met
questions.explanation: Filters stop the flow unless the condition is satisfied.

questions.question: When you need to process each item in a list, you use...
questions.explanation: Loops/iterators process each item in a collection one at a time.

questions.question: A 'delay' step is used to...
options: Wait a set time before the next action
questions.explanation: Delays pause the workflow (e.g. wait 1 hour) before continuing.

questions.question: 'Mapping' data between steps means...
options: Passing output fields of one step into another step's inputs
questions.explanation: Mapping connects one step's outputs to the next step's inputs.

questions.question: By default, steps in a simple workflow run...
questions.explanation: Steps execute sequentially unless branching/parallel logic is added.

questions.question: An API is occasionally down. How do you make the workflow reliable?
options: Add retries with a delay and an error-handling path
questions.explanation: Retries with backoff plus an error branch make automations resilient.

questions.question: To avoid processing the same record twice, you should...
options: Track processed IDs / use deduplication logic
questions.explanation: Storing processed IDs (dedup) prevents duplicate handling of the same item.

questions.question: When are parallel branches preferable to sequential steps?
options: When independent tasks can run at the same time to save time
questions.explanation: Independent tasks can run in parallel branches, reducing total run time.
